title: Walk the three-level lookup hierarchy when choosing each form's definition source
description: For every form the protocol requires, the skill walks down a three-level hierarchy and stops at the first level with a match: (1) Customer OC4 XLSForm Standard — sponsor-level reusable templates maintained at the customer level, matched by form_id / CDASH domain / explicit form_name token; (2) Customer CRF Library — study-specific files supplied with the protocol upload, matched by filename token / CDASH domain / explicit form_name token; (3) CDASH form definition — the skill's built-in default fallback from references/cdash-domain-library.md. The output spec records `definition_source` per form (`customer_oc4_standard` / `customer_crf_library` / `cdash_default`). Many downstream conventions filter by this attribute — they apply only to fields generated at level 3 (CDASH defaults) and leave customer-sourced content as-authored.
rationale: Sponsors invest in reusable form templates so they don't redefine the same AE/CM/VS/LB forms for every study. The hierarchy gives those investments priority over generic CDASH defaults. The three-level walk also makes the decision auditable — every form's spec includes the level that produced it, so reviewers can trace why a form looks the way it does. Conventions §3, §4, §5, §13, §14 and others explicitly gate themselves on `definition_source: cdash_default` for the same reason: customer-sourced content has its own logic and shouldn't be silently overridden by skill-default conventions.

history.note: Imported from conventions.md §0.B during Phase B.1d. Advisory because the rule documents the skill's form-definition selection process, which happens before the engine evaluates per-field conventions. Many downstream §-rules filter on the `definition_source` attribute this rule populates — it is the foundational dependency for the §0-scoped conventions throughout the rest of System 2. The three levels conceptually parallel the conventions engine's own cascade (global / customer / study) but operate at the form-definition layer, not the conventions layer.
